title: Anjimile's Musical Rebirth: Vulnerability and Transformation
content:
Anjimile's latest album, 'You're Free to Go,' was indirectly influenced by his recent, life-changing move to Durham, North Carolina. His producer, Cook, clicked instantly with him and hoped to make a record reflecting the person he was getting to know. Cook described the songs as some of the bravest and most uncompromising writing he had ever encountered in his career. He noted that Anjimile beautifully articulates the experience of being a Black trans man in America with spiritual calm. The primary challenge during production was ensuring that other facets of his vibrant personality also shined through the music.

In conversation, Anjimile often punctuates quiet, somber reflections with tension-piercing quips and infectious laughter. He speaks thoughtfully about heavy subjects like suicidal ideation, but also excitedly discusses teenage skateboarding and 1990s Disney movies. Rebirth and transformation serve as recurring themes throughout this deeply personal and highly anticipated record. For example, the title track opens with an airy guitar melody and the lyrics, 'There is something like a new being growing on me.' Other tracks expertly balance this emotional vulnerability with pure, unadulterated pop beats and warm synths.

The album's distinct sound was heavily inspired by late-90s and early-00s alternative pop-rock melodies. Anjimile readily admits to drawing inspiration from nostalgic acts like Third Eye Blind, Alien Ant Farm, and Duncan Sheik. He listened to these nostalgic melodies frequently in the year following his painful estrangement from his mother. Musician Sam Beam, who provided backing vocals, praised the resulting tracks as intricate, beautiful, and profoundly vulnerable. Ultimately, this album marks a triumphant new chapter for Anjimile as he fearlessly moves forward in his recovery.
